title: Writing on calfskin vellum
subtitle: How the surface, the ink, and the pen all change on skin
excerpt: Calfskin vellum is more sensitive and less forgiving than any paper. Ink behaves differently, mistakes are harder to correct, and the surface reacts visibly to humidity. This tutorial covers preparing the surface, choosing the right ink and pen combination, and recovering from the small disasters that vellum reliably presents.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on vellum preparation and writing, Johnston worked predominantly on calfskin vellum and gives detailed preparation and working advice. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: Calfskin (or occasionally lambskin or goatskin) prepared by scraping thin while wet on a wooden frame and drying under tension. The traditional writing surface for illuminated manuscripts.
Rubbing the surface of vellum with powdered sandarac resin before writing to reduce ink spread and improve the crispness of the written edge.
The buckling or waving of vellum caused by changes in humidity; the skin absorbs moisture from the air and expands unevenly.

content.text: is animal skin, the same calfskin used for drum heads, dried and scraped to translucency. It has no grain, no deckle, no fibre texture. Under the pen it feels slippery and alive.

is its characteristic failure mode: a piece of vellum left in a humid room overnight may be completely distorted by morning. Working with it requires respect for humidity, for the surface preparation, and for the ink.

items.name: Calfskin vellum, at least A5, mounted on a board with tape (see below)
items.substitutions: Goatskin vellum; for practice, a very smooth hot-press watercolour paper 300 gsm

items.name: Gum sandarac (powdered)
items.qty: a small amount in a muslin pouncing bag

items.name: Iron-gall ink (modern formulation)
items.substitutions: Avoid carbon-based inks (sumi) on vellum, they sit on the surface without bonding and can lift during gilding

content.text: A loose piece of vellum will cockle as soon as you start writing. Attach it to a board before work begins. Wet the vellum lightly on the flesh (rough) side with a barely-damp sponge, and immediately tape all four edges to a smooth board with low-tack masking tape. As it dries, the vellum will tighten against the board. Work on the hair (smooth, writing) side.

The vellum surface is naturally greasy. A light dusting of powdered gum sandarac rubbed in circular motions with a pouncing bag dries the surface oils and makes the ink settle crisply rather than bleeding. Too much sandarac is as bad as too little, it will repel the ink entirely. A single light dusting is sufficient.

Iron-gall ink is the correct choice for vellum writing. It bonds chemically with the skin surface rather than simply coating it. Use a slightly stiffer nib than you would on paper, a Mitchell Roundhand 3 rather than a 4 or 5, because vellum has more drag than paper and a soft nib will skip.

Always rest your writing hand on a tracing-paper guard, not directly on the vellum. The natural oils from the hand will repel the ink in the areas you have touched, causing the ink to bead rather than flowing as a smooth stroke.

Wait until the ink is completely dry, at least 30 minutes. Scrape lightly with a clean, very sharp craft knife, held almost flat against the surface at a low angle. The vellum will scrape white dust rather than cutting fibres. After scraping, re-pounce the patch before writing over it.

items.symptom: Ink beads on the surface instead of flowing
items.cause: Grease on the vellum surface, either natural skin oils or hand contact.
items.fix: Re-pounce the affected area with sandarac and try again. Do not touch the surface directly.

items.symptom: Vellum cockles severely even when mounted
items.cause: Humidity in the room is too high, or the wet-mounting was not secure enough.
items.fix: Work in the driest room available. Re-wet and re-mount with the tape closer to the edges.

items.symptom: Scraping tears the surface instead of removing ink
items.cause: The knife is too thick-angled, or the ink was not completely dry.
items.fix: Wait longer before scraping. The knife angle should be nearly flat, almost parallel to the surface.
